intro: Cycling performance training relies on solid physiological principles: adaptation, supercompensation, and specificity. Power zones, weekly training load, and recovery are the pillars of performance gains. FTP (Functional Threshold Power) is the best single metric for establishing these zones. Calculate your FTP from the 20-minute field test protocol—fast, free, and based on the standard cycling model. Updated for 2026 with periodic reviews to keep you current.
keyTakeaway: Formula: **FTP = average_watts_20min × 0.95**. Direct application with your test data. Calculate your FTP from your 20-minute test effort instantly.

fields.label: Average Watts (20-min test)
fields.help: Your average power output in watts during the 20-minute test.

explanation:
Cycling performance training combines science (physiology, biomechanics) with practical application. Power zones, progressive overload, recovery, and nutrition form the complete training system.

## How FTP Is Calculated

The 20-minute field test is a practical way to estimate your FTP. You ride all-out for 20 minutes while holding a steady pace (not sprinting, not pacing yourself). The average watts during this 20-minute effort represent your sustainable power output. We multiply by 0.95 because 20 minutes is shorter than the theoretical 60-minute threshold effort—cyclists can maintain slightly higher power for the shorter duration.

This calculator applies the formula to your test data, flagging unusually high or low values.

## Final Notes

This calculation provides a reference estimate for training purposes. For critical decisions (health, medical, or performance-related), consult with a certified cycling coach or sports scientist. Data is current as of 2026 and reviewed periodically.

faq.a: FTP is the maximum power you can sustain for one hour. It's the most important metric for setting cycling training zones and measuring fitness progress.
A 20-minute effort allows you to maintain slightly higher power than your true 1-hour threshold because it's shorter. The 0.95 factor adjusts for this physiological difference.

faq.q: What are typical FTP power levels for cyclists?
faq.a: Beginner: <2 W/kg. Amateur: 2.5–3.5 W/kg. Advanced: 4–5 W/kg. Elite: >5.5 W/kg. (Values are watts per kilogram of body weight.)

faq.q: How often should I test my FTP?
faq.a: Test every 6–8 weeks during training blocks. Ensure you're well-rested 2–3 days before the test for accurate results.

faq.q: How can I improve my FTP?
faq.a: Build with sweet-spot intervals (88–94% FTP), VO2-max repeats, and consistent base miles. Progressive overload and adequate recovery are essential.

faq.q: Can I use this calculator on my phone?
faq.a: Yes. All our calculators work on mobile browsers with no app required. Calculations happen locally in your device.
Completely. All calculations run in your browser. We don't send any data to our servers or store your results.

faq.q: How accurate is this FTP estimate?
faq.a: The formula is accurate within ±3–5% for well-executed tests. Use flat terrain, steady effort, no interruptions, and good pacing discipline.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data update required
